title: Unit Final: Personal Finance and Consumer Economics

questions.prompt: Which action is the best example of budgeting?

choices.text: Spending money without tracking expenses
choices.feedback: This does not show budgeting because budgeting requires planning and tracking money.

choices.text: Making a plan for income, saving, and monthly expenses
choices.feedback: Correct. Budgeting means planning how income will be used for expenses, saving, and financial goals.

choices.text: Using credit cards for every purchase
choices.feedback: Using credit can be part of spending, but it does not define budgeting.

choices.text: Avoiding all purchases, including necessary ones
choices.feedback: A budget helps balance needs, wants, and saving. It does not mean avoiding all spending.

questions.prompt: Saving money for emergencies can improve financial security.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Emergency savings help people handle unexpected costs without taking on unnecessary debt.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Emergency savings are an important part of financial security because they help cover unplanned expenses.

questions.prompt: What is the main difference between saving and investing?

choices.text: Saving is only for businesses, while investing is only for individuals
choices.feedback: Both individuals and businesses can save or invest.

choices.text: Saving is usually lower risk, while investing often involves greater risk for possible growth
choices.feedback: Correct. Saving is generally safer, while investing usually involves more risk but can offer higher returns over time.

choices.text: Saving always earns more money than investing
choices.feedback: Investing may earn more over time, although it also involves more risk.

choices.text: Investing means spending money on wants instead of needs
choices.feedback: Investing means putting money into assets with the goal of future growth, not simply spending.

questions.prompt: A student gets paid every two weeks and notices they keep running out of money before payday. Which action would most likely improve their situation?

choices.text: Track expenses and create a budget that separates needs, wants, and savings
choices.feedback: Correct. Tracking expenses and using a budget helps control spending and plan for the full pay period.

choices.text: Ignore the spending pattern and hope next month is different
choices.feedback: Ignoring the problem does not address why money is running out before payday.

choices.text: Use borrowed money for all purchases without a repayment plan
choices.feedback: Borrowing without a plan can create debt problems instead of solving the budgeting issue.

choices.text: Stop paying for all necessary expenses
choices.feedback: A strong budget prioritizes necessary expenses rather than avoiding them.

questions.prompt: Borrowing money always means a person is making an irresponsible financial decision.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Borrowing is not always irresponsible. What matters is whether the borrower understands the terms and can repay the debt responsibly.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Correct. Borrowing can be responsible if the loan terms are understood and the debt can be repaid on time.

questions.prompt: Which choice best shows responsible use of credit?

choices.text: Taking out multiple loans without comparing interest rates
choices.feedback: Responsible credit use includes understanding and comparing borrowing costs.

choices.text: Ignoring monthly statements and payment due dates
choices.feedback: Ignoring statements and due dates can lead to missed payments and more debt.

choices.text: Borrowing only what can be repaid and making payments on time
choices.feedback: Correct. Responsible credit use means borrowing carefully and repaying debt as agreed.

choices.text: Choosing the first loan offered without reading the terms
choices.feedback: Loan terms should be reviewed carefully before borrowing.

questions.prompt: A person is choosing between two laptops. One costs less now but has poor reviews and no warranty. The other costs more but has stronger reviews and a warranty. Which factor is most directly part of consumer decision-making in this situation?

choices.text: Evaluating value and financial risk before purchasing
choices.feedback: Correct. Consumer decision-making includes comparing value, quality, and financial risk, not just price.

choices.text: Avoiding all purchases that cost more upfront
choices.feedback: Paying more can sometimes be the stronger choice if it reduces risk and improves long-term value.

choices.text: Assuming the lowest price is always the best decision
choices.feedback: Good consumer decisions consider more than the lowest immediate price.

choices.text: Using credit automatically without comparing options
choices.feedback: Automatic borrowing is not the main decision-making strategy being evaluated here.

questions.prompt: Interest is the cost of borrowing money.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Interest is the amount paid for the use of borrowed money.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Interest is the cost of borrowing, so this statement is true.

questions.prompt: A student wants to build financial stability over time. Which plan best combines ideas from budgeting, saving, and debt responsibility?

choices.text: Spend all income each month and borrow for emergencies
choices.feedback: This approach does not support budgeting, saving, or responsible debt management.

choices.text: Create a budget, save part of each paycheck, and avoid borrowing more than can be repaid
choices.feedback: Correct. This plan combines budgeting, saving, and responsible borrowing into one strong financial strategy.

choices.text: Ignore expenses as long as bills are sometimes paid
choices.feedback: Financial stability requires consistent planning, not ignoring expenses.

choices.text: Focus only on buying the cheapest products, even if quality is poor
choices.feedback: Price matters, but financial stability also depends on budgeting, saving, and managing debt responsibly.

questions.prompt: Which situation best shows financial risk in consumer decision-making?

choices.text: Comparing prices before choosing a product
choices.feedback: Comparing prices is generally a good strategy for reducing risk.

choices.text: Reading reviews before making a purchase
choices.feedback: Reading reviews helps evaluate value and reduce uncertainty.

choices.text: Buying an expensive item without checking quality, terms, or total cost
choices.feedback: Correct. Ignoring quality, terms, and total cost increases financial risk.

choices.text: Saving money before making a large purchase
choices.feedback: Saving before a major purchase usually reduces financial risk.
